metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the What section the names and semantics of every new LLMUnavailableError subclass to add to errors.py (or explicitly state that errors.py is NOT modified in this story and remove it from the file list + AC3's isinstance claim).
findings.finding: The Scope section and AC3 reference new typed error subclasses of LLMUnavailableError (in errors.py), but the What section never names, describes, or specifies the semantics of these new classes — an executor cannot implement them without inventing names and behavior.
findings.location: Scope bullet: 'The closed 8-class enum + … typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError) … src/rebar/llm/errors.py (typed subclasses)'; AC3: 'New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError; a test… asserts… every existing except LLMUnavailableError still catches the new subclasses'
findings.suggested_fix: If the new exception subclasses are a forward-definition for blackbear to raise, list their names explicitly (e.g. `class LLMTransientError(LLMUnavailableError): ...`, one per retryable bucket) and note they are defined-but-not-yet-raised. If no new exceptions belong in this story, remove `src/rebar/llm/errors.py` from the file list and reword AC3(a) to verify only that the existing LLMUnavailableError catch still works.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `errors.py` gains any new exception classes (and name them), or remove the 'typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' language from Scope and 'New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError' from AC-3 if no new exceptions are introduced.
findings.finding: The plan names 'New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError' in AC-3 and 'the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' in Scope as an edit target for `errors.py`, but the 'What' section never names any specific new exception class. The plan defines `ResolutionClass`, `LLMOutcome`, `ClassifyContext`, `classify_llm_failure`, and `sanitize_diagnostic` — none of which are exception subclasses. It is unclear what exception classes are actually being added to `errors.py`.
findings.location: Scope section ('the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)') and AC-3 ('New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError')
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is purely classification-metadata (no new raised types), remove the 'typed error hierarchy' bullet from Scope and reframe AC-3(a) as: 'every existing `except LLMUnavailableError` still catches exceptions classified by `classify_llm_failure` (isinstance holds for `LLMUnavailableError` and `LLMError`).' If new subclasses ARE intended, name them explicitly (e.g. `RateLimitError`, `ProviderAuthError`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the lifecycle of the new `LLMUnavailableError` typed subclasses: either (a) explicitly state they are forward-declarations for blackbear/2eca wiring and are NOT raised anywhere in this story (and remove them from this story's `errors.py` scope), or (b) specify exactly which seam raises them in this story and reconcile with the no-raised-type constraint.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously mandates 'New typed errors subclass `LLMUnavailableError`' in `errors.py` (Files section and AC-3) AND forbids any raised-type change at any seam in this story. The typed subclasses are therefore never raised anywhere in this story's scope — they are dead-code symbols. The plan does not clarify whether these subclasses should be raised within this story (contradicting the no-raised-type constraint) or are purely forward-declarations for blackbear/2eca to wire. An implementer cannot resolve this ambiguity from the plan text, risking either: (a) genuinely unreachable subclasses that satisfy AC-3 only via isinstance assertions that are never actually triggered by live code paths, or (b) accidental wiring that breaks the `sizing.py` ladder's `except LLMUnavailableError: raise` re-raise contract.
findings.location: Files section ('src/rebar/llm/errors.py (typed subclasses)') and AC-3 ('New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError') vs 'Metadata-only integration in THIS story' section ('CHANGES NO RAISED TYPE at those seams')
findings.suggested_fix: If these typed subclasses are only needed by blackbear/2eca, remove `src/rebar/llm/errors.py` from this story's Files list and move the subclass definitions to blackbear's scope. If they are intended for this story, name exactly one seam where a subclass is raised and confirm `sizing.py`'s `except LLMUnavailableError: raise` still propagates it correctly (it will, by isinstance — but the plan should state this explicitly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge explicitly in the mapping table or integration section that `UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` classification requires either (a) also attaching `LLMOutcome` at the dedicated `UsageLimitExceeded` arm (runner.py:335-350) where it is re-raised as `LLMRunnerError`, or (b) documenting that this row is only callable directly (not via the runner's generic seam) and that attaching it to the `LLMRunnerError` at line 346 is blackbear/2eca's responsibility.
findings.finding: The `UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` row in the mapping table is structurally unreachable via the only seam where `classify_llm_failure` is wired in this story (the generic `except Exception` arm at runner.py:353-365). `UsageLimitExceeded` is intercepted at the DEDICATED prior arm (runner.py:335) and re-raised as `LLMRunnerError` — which is then caught by `except LLMError: raise` (line 351) and passes through unchanged, never reaching the generic arm. The classifier at the generic seam therefore can never observe a `UsageLimitExceeded` instance. The RED suite test that pins this row will need to call `classify_llm_failure` DIRECTLY (bypassing the runner) — which is valid but the plan does not state this, leaving the test strategy ambiguous.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table row 'UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN' vs 'Metadata-only integration in THIS story' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the mapping table: 'UsageLimitExceeded is caught at runner.py:335 before the generic seam — the classifier maps this signal correctly when called directly, but attaching outcome to the LLMRunnerError raised there is out of scope for this story (blackbear/2eca).' Alternatively, extend the metadata-only runner touch to also attach LLMOutcome at line 346.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'one-off empty/parse blip' trigger in the RETRY_NOW mapping row with the concrete exception type(s) from pydantic-ai/httpx that represent empty or malformed responses (e.g. a specific `ModelAPIError` message pattern or an `httpx.RemoteProtocolError`).
findings.finding: The RETRY_NOW row includes 'one-off empty/parse blip' as a named trigger but provides no exception type, message pattern, or detectable attribute — an executor has no concrete artifact to match in the classifier.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table, row: 'httpx.ConnectError/ConnectTimeout; ModelAPIError (no status); one-off empty/parse blip → RETRY_NOW'

findings.finding: `UsageLimitExceeded` is caught and re-wrapped as `LLMRunnerError` at runner.py:335-350 BEFORE the `except Exception` seam where `classify_llm_failure` is planned to sit, so the plan's `UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` mapping is mechanically unreachable as specified: the classifier will never see a raw `UsageLimitExceeded`.
findings.location: What section — Exception→resolution_class mapping table / runner.py:335-350
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' or 'Scope' section, add: 'The existing `except UsageLimitExceeded` handler in runner.py:335-350 is folded into the classifier — it is removed and `UsageLimitExceeded` is allowed to fall through to the `except Exception` classifier seam, which maps it to FIX_AGENT_DESIGN and raises the typed error. The step-budget log line is preserved inside the classifier's FIX_AGENT_DESIGN branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete description in the 'What' section of the new typed error subclasses: enumerate their names, which resolution_class each corresponds to (if any), and clarify whether they are raised in this story or only defined for future use.
findings.finding: The 'What' section enumerates exactly five new constructs (ResolutionClass, LLMOutcome, ClassifyContext, classify_llm_failure, sanitize_diagnostic) but does NOT describe what the 'new typed errors subclassing LLMUnavailableError' actually are — their names, count, or purpose. AC3 tests these subclasses (isinstance, behavior preservation) but there is no corresponding description of the subclasses in the What section, leaving the deliverable under-specified.
findings.location: What section (five constructs listed) vs Scope ('the typed error hierarchy') vs AC3 ('New typed errors subclass LLMUnavailableError')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether bare `UnexpectedModelBehavior` (not a subclass) is raised for exhausted output-validation retries and, if so, add it to the CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL row of the mapping table; confirm whether `FallbackExceptionGroup` is the correct signal for multi-model fallback exhaustion vs single-model retry exhaustion (a one-line offline probe of pydantic-ai's exhausted-retry path would settle this).
findings.finding: The mapping table routes 'validation-retry exhausted' to CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL via FallbackExceptionGroup, but the project's own structured-output research doc records that exhausted pydantic-ai output-validation retries raise `UnexpectedModelBehavior: Exceeded maximum retries (N) for output validation` — the BASE class, not FallbackExceptionGroup. FallbackExceptionGroup appears to be for multi-model fallback group failures, not single-model retry exhaustion. The plan says 'discriminate UnexpectedModelBehavior subclasses (ContentFilterError, IncompleteToolCall) rather than the base', which implies bare UnexpectedModelBehavior is left unmatched — falling through to NEEDS_INVESTIGATION instead of CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL. If the retry-exhausted path actually raises base UnexpectedModelBehavior, it is systematically misclassified.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table (CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL row); 'Verified library behavior' experiment log [1]
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate mapping row: bare `UnexpectedModelBehavior` (i.e. `isinstance(exc, UnexpectedModelBehavior) and not isinstance(exc, (ContentFilterError, IncompleteToolCall))`) → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, retryable=False. Keep FallbackExceptionGroup → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL for the multi-model-fallback case. Then the experiment log's [1] MRO evidence for both types is used correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan whether an LLMOutcome is attached to the LLMRunnerError at the UsageLimitExceeded arm (runner.py:335-350), or confirm that LLMOutcome attachment there is intentionally deferred to blackbear/2eca — and add an AC that pins this either way.
findings.finding: The plan's classifier mapping table assigns UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, implying classify_llm_failure will receive a UsageLimitExceeded. But runner.py:335 catches UsageLimitExceeded BEFORE the except Exception seam and re-raises it as LLMRunnerError. The metadata-only runner touch attaches LLMOutcome only at the except Exception seam (runner.py:353-365), so the LLMRunnerError raised from the UsageLimitExceeded path will have NO LLMOutcome attached. The plan partially acknowledges leaving that arm as-is but does not acknowledge this outcome-attachment gap.
findings.location: Section 'What' — 'Metadata-only integration in THIS story' and mapping table row 'UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single line to the UsageLimitExceeded arm: `err = LLMRunnerError(...); err.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc); raise err` — consistent with metadata-only (no type change) and gives the FIX_AGENT_DESIGN classification a path to the raised error.

findings.finding: The plan's classifier mapping assigns `UsageLimitExceeded -> FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` at 'the single runner except-seam', but `UsageLimitExceeded` is caught by a PRIOR dedicated arm at runner.py:335-350 that converts it to `LLMRunnerError` (which subclasses `LLMError`, not `LLMUnavailableError`) and re-raises it via the `except LLMError: raise` arm at line 351 — meaning `UsageLimitExceeded` NEVER reaches the bare `except Exception` seam at line 353 where `classify_llm_failure` is to be inserted. The classifier, as scoped to the single except-seam, will never see a raw `UsageLimitExceeded`.
findings.location: Plan section 'Exception → resolution_class mapping' table row: `UsageLimitExceeded (request/tool_calls limit) … -> FIX_AGENT_DESIGN`; cross-referenced with runner.py:335-352
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a `classify_llm_failure` call (or equivalent `FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` outcome construction) inside the existing `except UsageLimitExceeded as exc:` arm at runner.py:335-350, alongside the existing `LLMRunnerError` raise — or restructure so the new typed `FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` error replaces the current `LLMRunnerError` raise there, preserving back-compat via subclassing.

findings.finding: The plan maps `UsageLimitExceeded` → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN in the classifier, but the existing `runner.py` already intercepts `UsageLimitExceeded` at line 335 and re-raises it as `LLMRunnerError` (which passes through the `except LLMError: raise` guard at line 351) — so the raw `UsageLimitExceeded` never reaches the `except Exception` seam where `classify_llm_failure` is to be inserted. As written, the classifier would never see a raw `UsageLimitExceeded`, making that mapping-table row dead code.
findings.location: runner.py lines 335–365; plan section 'What' / exception→resolution_class mapping table (UsageLimitExceeded row)
findings.suggested_fix: Move `classify_llm_failure` to run as the outermost catch (before the existing `except UsageLimitExceeded`) so it receives the raw `UsageLimitExceeded` directly, OR remove the dedicated `except UsageLimitExceeded` block and let `classify_llm_failure` handle it as part of the pydantic-ai exception taxonomy.

findings.finding: The existing `except UsageLimitExceeded` handler at `runner.py:335` catches `UsageLimitExceeded` and re-raises it as `LLMRunnerError` BEFORE the generic `except Exception` block where the classifier sits. As a result, raw `UsageLimitExceeded` never reaches the classifier, making the mapping table entry `UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` structurally unreachable unless the pre-existing handler is removed or refactored. The plan does not describe this interaction or how to handle it.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table row 'UsageLimitExceeded (request/tool_calls limit)'; runner.py:335–350

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'one-off empty/parse blip' in the RETRY_NOW mapping row with the specific Python exception type(s) (e.g. `json.JSONDecodeError`, a named pydantic-ai parse exception, or 'none — drop this signal') so the executor and RED suite agree on the concrete type.
findings.finding: The mapping table row 'one-off empty/parse blip' → RETRY_NOW does not name the concrete exception type(s) that constitute a 'blip', leaving the executor to guess whether this means json.JSONDecodeError, a pydantic ValidationError, an empty-string body from the HTTP layer, or some pydantic-ai error.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table, RETRY_NOW row: 'ModelAPIError (no status); one-off empty/parse blip'
findings.suggested_fix: If pydantic-ai wraps parse failures inside ModelAPIError already, drop the 'blip' sub-bullet and note that. Otherwise name the type: e.g. 'json.JSONDecodeError raised by the provider response parser'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the file-placement contradiction: specify ONE canonical home for the new typed error subclasses (failure.py or errors.py) and remove the conflicting reference.
findings.finding: The 'Scope' section places 'the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' in `src/rebar/llm/failure.py`, but the 'Files' section assigns typed subclasses to `src/rebar/llm/errors.py`. An implementer following Scope would create them in failure.py; one following Files would create them in errors.py.
findings.location: Scope section ('all in src/rebar/llm/failure.py') vs Files section ('src/rebar/llm/errors.py (typed subclasses)')
findings.suggested_fix: Move 'typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' out of the failure.py parenthetical in Scope, and add a sentence: 'New typed subclasses are defined in errors.py and imported by failure.py for mapping purposes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete exception type or detection heuristic for the 'schema/tooling unsatisfiable' CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL trigger (e.g. `UnexpectedModelBehavior` with a specific message pattern, or a distinct pydantic-ai symbol), co-located in the mapping table.
findings.finding: The CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL row lists 'schema/tooling unsatisfiable' as a trigger signal but names no concrete exception type, body code, or detectable attribute — an executor implementing the classifier cannot match it.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table, row: 'validation-retry exhausted; schema/tooling unsatisfiable; FallbackExceptionGroup → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'schema/tooling unsatisfiable' with 'UnexpectedModelBehavior whose message matches "Exceeded maximum retries" (validation-retry exhaustion)' — the experiment log already confirms this is the pydantic-ai signal for exhausted output-validation retries. If a separate 'schema unsatisfiable' signal exists, name its exception type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via experiment or pydantic-ai source inspection) that FallbackExceptionGroup is actually raised through rebar's runner agent loop AND that it is caught by `except Exception` (not requiring `except*`), or scope the FallbackExceptionGroup row as best-effort/unverified.
findings.finding: The plan's exception-to-resolution_class mapping table lists 'FallbackExceptionGroup' as a signal for CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, citing it as a pydantic-ai symbol. The rebar codebase has zero references to FallbackExceptionGroup (no imports, no catches, no mentions), so there is no verified path by which FallbackExceptionGroup currently reaches the except Exception seam in runner.py. The plan's experiment log confirms the symbol exists in pydantic-ai but makes no claim about whether it is actually raised and propagates through rebar's runner in practice.
findings.location: Section 'Exception → resolution_class mapping' — 'validation-retry exhausted; schema/tooling unsatisfiable; FallbackExceptionGroup → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section exactly which new subclasses are added to errors.py and whether they are raised anywhere (or whether the 'typed error hierarchy' bullet refers only to the LLMOutcome dataclass in failure.py, not new exception subclasses).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Scope' section states 'The closed 8-class enum + LLMOutcome + ClassifyContext + the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)... all in src/rebar/llm/failure.py' and names errors.py for 'typed subclasses'. However, the 'What' section and the metadata-only runner touch both prohibit changing raised types. No raised type at any seam is changed, so these new LLMUnavailableError subclasses would exist in errors.py but never be raised — making the stated rationale for subclassing contradictory with the metadata-only constraint.
findings.location: Section 'Scope' — 'typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' and 'Files: ... src/rebar/llm/errors.py (typed subclasses)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan title (or add a title footnote) to replace 'verbatim diagnostic' with 'sanitized diagnostic' (or 'allowlist-restricted diagnostic') so the title accurately reflects the sanitization-first design in the body.
findings.finding: The title promises a 'verbatim diagnostic' but the body explicitly delivers a sanitized, allowlist-restricted diagnostic — raw provider bodies are never dumped wholesale, free-text messages are redacted, and secrets are masked. 'Verbatim' and 'sanitized/allowlist' are contradictory; the title will mislead implementers and downstream story authors who rely on it as a contract summary.
findings.location: Plan title vs. 'Diagnostic sanitization' section and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Rename title to: 'Classify LLM failures into the resolution-disposition enum + sanitized diagnostic' — or add a parenthetical: '(sanitized; secrets redacted)' to prevent contract confusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope and AC3 whether the 'typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' is (a) merely the new typed errors used as classifier INPUT signal discrimination (already handled by the mapping table), or (b) new raise-site subclasses — and if (b), add an explicit raise site within this story's scope or defer the subclass definitions to blackbear (2eca) where the raise sites live.
findings.finding: The Scope lists 'the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)' as a deliverable of this story, but the plan's What section and Acceptance Criteria never specify a code path where any new subclass is RAISED in this story. The minimal runner touch attaches LLMOutcome to the existing LLMUnavailableError without raising a new subclass; no other seam in this story raises new types by design ('CHANGES NO RAISED TYPE at those seams'). Defining subclasses that are never raised in this story is scope that serves a future story (blackbear/2eca) rather than this one's stated goal of a pure classification vocabulary.
findings.location: Scope section: 'The closed 8-class enum + LLMOutcome + ClassifyContext + the typed error hierarchy (subclassing LLMUnavailableError)'; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the minimum length threshold for 'generic long base64/hex secrets' in the sanitizer spec (e.g. ≥32 contiguous base64/hex chars) so the regex is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The sanitizer spec says the pattern table masks 'generic long base64/hex secrets' but does not define what 'long' means (minimum length threshold), leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary cutoff that may under-redact or over-redact diagnostic fields.
findings.location: Diagnostic sanitization section: 'key-shaped tokens (`sk-...`, `sk-ant-...`, generic long base64/hex secrets)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sanitizer section: 'base64/hex runs of ≥32 characters that are not otherwise structured data (e.g. not a UUID) are treated as secrets and redacted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 how the test discovers all except-LLMUnavailableError / exit-code consumers (e.g. 'grep -rn LLMUnavailableError src/ to produce the reference list, then assert each still catches via isinstance') so the enumeration step is reproducible and bounded.
findings.finding: AC3 says 'a test enumerates the existing except LLMUnavailableError / exit-code consumers and asserts BEHAVIOR PRESERVED' but gives no guidance on how the test discovers or bounds the set of consumers — a hardcoded list risks silently missing a consumer added before this story ships.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'a test enumerates the existing except LLMUnavailableError / exit-code consumers and asserts BEHAVIOR PRESERVED'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prose note: 'The test constructs the consumer list by running `grep -rn "except LLMUnavailableError" src/` and asserting the count matches a pinned baseline N; any new consumer must update the baseline, making additions explicit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the tenacity RetryError regression test is required to RUN (and pass) in wave-1: if tenacity is absent, specify whether the test is skipped via pytest.importorskip or tenacity is added as a dev dependency now; resolve the contradiction between 'wave-1 still passes' and the mandatory regression.
findings.finding: Tenacity is described as guarded with `try/except ImportError` so 'wave-1 (no tenacity) still passes', but AC2 mandates a concrete regression test that 'injects a RetryError wrapping an httpx.ReadTimeout and asserts WAIT_AND_RETRY.' These two statements contradict: if tenacity is absent in wave-1 the test cannot import or instantiate `tenacity.RetryError` and must be skipped or will error, yet AC2 asserts it passes.
findings.location: What section (tenacity guard paragraph) vs AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a module-level docstring to `failure.py` capturing the public API contract (enum semantics, sanitizer allowlist rationale, classifier guarantees) as the in-code authoritative reference that serves downstream consumers before blackbear's ADR ships; optionally note the story-ordering dependency (this story → blackbear ADR → drake wiring).
findings.finding: The ADR for the novel closed 8-class vocabulary and sanitizer allowlist is explicitly deferred to story blackbear (2eca) with no stated ordering constraint relative to story drake, which will already import from `failure.py`. If blackbear ships after drake, the authoritative design rationale is absent when downstream consumers need it — and if blackbear ships before this story, the ADR may be written against a not-yet-final API. More critically, `failure.py` is described as a "stable path" inter-child contract but the plan specifies no in-story documentation (module docstring, public-API contract header) that would serve as a working contract for downstream consumers before the ADR exists.
findings.location: Scope section / 'stable path; story drake imports from here' / 'the ADR is RECORDED by blackbear (2eca, sole ADR/docs owner)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `src/rebar/llm/failure.py` that documents: (1) the 8-class enum semantics and the rationale for the closed vocabulary, (2) the sanitizer allowlist fields and the no-raw-body guarantee, (3) the classifier's total/never-raises contract, and (4) a note that `err.outcome` is metadata-only and does not change raised types. This gives drake a working contract independent of the ADR timeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly verify or document that `LLMUnavailableError` and its new subclasses in `errors.py` permit dynamic attribute assignment (no `__slots__` conflict), OR wrap the `err.outcome = outcome` assignment in a `try/except AttributeError` that silently skips attachment rather than propagating.
findings.finding: The plan directs `err.outcome = outcome` to be set on a live `LLMUnavailableError` instance inside the except handler in runner.py, but does not specify whether `LLMUnavailableError` (or its new subclasses) permit dynamic attribute assignment. If the class uses `__slots__` without an `outcome` slot, this assignment raises `AttributeError` inside the except handler, potentially suppressing or corrupting the original exception flow — the exact opposite of the plan's 'change no raised type' guarantee.
findings.location: runner.py metadata-only touch / 'attach the resulting LLMOutcome to the raised LLMUnavailableError as an attribute (e.g. err.outcome)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `errors.py`, either ensure the base class does not use restrictive `__slots__`, OR in runner.py wrap the attachment: `try: err.outcome = outcome\nexcept AttributeError: pass` so a slot mismatch degrades silently rather than corrupting the handler.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `logging.warning` (or equivalent structured log) inside the sanitizer-failure catch path in `classify_llm_failure` so that sanitizer degradation is observable in production logs, even if full telemetry wiring is deferred to the downstream story.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a sanitizer failure degrades to a 'redaction-failed placeholder' and is AC-tested, but provides no instrumentation (log line, metric, or trace event) for this degraded path. In production, silent sanitizer failures would be invisible to operators, making it impossible to detect systematic redaction breakdowns without inspecting exception attributes.
findings.location: Diagnostic sanitization section / AC: 'sanitize_diagnostic failing does not make the classifier raise (it degrades to a placeholder)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the internal sanitizer-failure catch block: `import logging; logging.getLogger(__name__).warning('sanitize_diagnostic failed; diagnostic redacted', exc_info=True)` — a single line that makes the degraded path observable without requiring the full telemetry pipeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `sanitize_diagnostic`'s pattern table should extract/reuse the existing `_SECRET_PATTERNS` from `lint.py` or document explicitly why a separate table is intentional (e.g., different redaction strategy: lint.py flags; failure.py scrubs).
findings.finding: The `sanitize_diagnostic` redactor rebuilds secret-masking pattern logic already present in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint.py` (`_SECRET_PATTERNS` + `_redact()`). The plan proposes a new independent pattern table (bearer tokens, `sk-...`, `sk-ant-...`, base64/hex) without specifying whether the existing patterns are reused or whether a shared utility is extracted. This is a partial NIH: both implement regex-pattern tables to mask secrets in strings.
findings.location: Diagnostic sanitization section; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint.py:33-55`
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a shared `_SECRET_PATTERNS` constant (or a `_scrub_secrets(s: str) -> str` helper) into a leaf module (e.g., `rebar.llm._secrets`) that both `lint.py` and `failure.py` import, so the pattern table has one owner.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that defining `ClassifyContext` now (with 0 active call-sites in this story) is intentional as a stable contract for story drake — if so, add an explicit note that its fields are derived from the runner's available locals at the generic seam; otherwise defer the full dataclass to blackbear/2eca.
findings.finding: `ClassifyContext` is introduced as a named dataclass but has exactly zero call-sites that populate it in this story's scope. The only seam touched (`runner.py:353-365` generic `except Exception`) does not have structured access to `model`, `provider`, `trace_id`, or `finish_reason` — only the string `ran_model` and `exc` are in scope. The remaining wiring is deferred to blackbear/2eca. Introducing the named `ClassifyContext` type now (rather than as part of blackbear's wiring) is a premature abstraction by the Rule-of-Three: it has 0 active call-sites in this story.
findings.location: What section — `ClassifyContext` dataclass definition; AC bullet 1 (`classify_llm_failure` mapping); Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC bullet 5: specify that the 'property test over arbitrary exceptions' should use the repo's existing seeded-random deterministic pattern (as in `test_properties.py`) rather than `hypothesis`, to avoid introducing an undeclared dependency.
findings.finding: The AC requires a 'property test over arbitrary exceptions' for the totality/never-raises guarantee, but the codebase has no `hypothesis` dependency and the existing property-test pattern (used in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/classify/test_properties.py`) explicitly avoids it in favour of seeded-random deterministic tests. Introducing `hypothesis` here would be a new dependency without a codebase precedent.
findings.location: AC bullet 5: 'The classifier is total and never raises (property test over arbitrary exceptions).'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'property test over arbitrary exceptions' with 'seeded-random totality test (consistent with repo pattern in test_properties.py): iterate over a large sample of exception types/subtypes and assert classify_llm_failure never raises'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct all 'sizing.py:NNN' citations in the plan to 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py:NNN' (or the importable module path `rebar.llm.plan_review.sizing`).
findings.finding: The plan cites 'sizing.py:171' and 'sizing.py:229' (in 'The multi-seam reality' section and AC) as if sizing.py is a root-level module, but the actual file is src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py. This is a verifiably false path assertion.
findings.location: Section 'The multi-seam reality' — 'sizing.is_context_limit_error(exc) (sizing.py:171)' and 'pass1_with_ladder (sizing.py:229)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'sizing.py:171' → 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py:171' and 'sizing.py:229' → 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py:229' throughout the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` to the AC-3 consumer enumeration (or explicitly note they are `except LLMError` — not `except LLMUnavailableError` — and explain why they're excluded from the exit-code preservation test).
findings.finding: The plan's AC-3 consumer enumeration ('a test enumerates the existing `except LLMUnavailableError` / exit-code consumers') omits `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (5 `except llm.LLMError` clauses with associated CLI exit behavior) and `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` (5 more `except LLMError` clauses) — callers OUTSIDE `src/rebar/llm/` that would be affected if new subclasses of `LLMUnavailableError` (< `LLMError`) changed exit behavior.
findings.location: AC-3 ('a test enumerates the existing except LLMUnavailableError / exit-code consumers and asserts BEHAVIOR PRESERVED')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `sanitize_diagnostic` should consolidate or explicitly reference the existing `_SECRET_PATTERNS` table in `lint.py` (and the Jira-fixture redactor), or document why a standalone pattern table in `failure.py` is intentionally scoped differently — preventing silent drift across three redactors.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a third credential-redaction utility (sanitize_diagnostic with its own bearer-token / sk-... / base64 / email pattern table) without acknowledging two existing redactors in the same codebase: `_SECRET_PATTERNS` + `_redact()` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint.py` and the layered redaction in `scripts/capture_jira_fixtures.py`. This risks pattern drift — the new redactor may miss token shapes already covered by the existing tables, or vice versa, and future maintainers must update three separate lists.
findings.location: Diagnostic sanitization section; src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint.py:33-55; scripts/capture_jira_fixtures.py:84-116
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) extract a shared `_CREDENTIAL_PATTERNS` constant into `src/rebar/llm/errors.py` or a new `src/rebar/llm/_secrets.py` leaf module that both `lint.py` and `failure.py` import, or (b) add a plan note explicitly scoping `sanitize_diagnostic` to LLM-provider-specific token shapes only (sk-*, bearer, Anthropic-specific) and calling out that workflow-secret shapes (GitHub/Slack/Stripe) are out of scope for this diagnostic context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the 400 context-length vs malformed-400 discrimination rule in the classifier spec: either state that `failure.py` replicates a documented subset of the context-limit message patterns inline (acceptable duplication), or that it calls a shared utility extracted from `sizing.py` — the choice must be explicit to keep `failure.py` as a pure module.
findings.finding: The mapping table has two rows that both cover HTTP 400 (CHANGE_SETTINGS: 'malformed 400'; CHANGE_INPUT: '400 context-length') but the classifier spec does not co-locate the decision rule for distinguishing them — specifically, whether to reuse/call `sizing.is_context_limit_error` or duplicate its message-matching logic inside `failure.py`.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table, rows CHANGE_SETTINGS ('malformed 400') and CHANGE_INPUT ('400 context-length'); 'What' section for failure.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the mapping table: 'For 400 status, check `ModelHTTPError.body` against the same context-length patterns as `sizing.is_context_limit_error` (inline copy in failure.py — do NOT import from sizing.py to preserve the pure-module property); any 400 that does not match → CHANGE_SETTINGS.'

findings.finding: Classifying a 400 context-length error as a typed `LLMUnavailableError` subclass (CHANGE_INPUT) will break the `sizing.py` model-escalation ladder, which relies on context-limit exceptions NOT being `LLMUnavailableError` so they reach `is_context_limit_error` via the `except Exception` branch — after this story, `sizing.py`'s `except LLMUnavailableError: raise` guards will surface them instead.
findings.location: What section — Exception→resolution_class mapping ('400 context-length → CHANGE_INPUT') / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py:229-233, 256-262

findings.finding: The mapping table places `content_filter` finish_reason → CHANGE_INPUT, but in the existing runner the `content_filter` stop-reason already raises `UnretryableOutputError` (a rebar `LLMError` subclass) via `check_stop_reason`, which the `except LLMError: raise` clause passes through BEFORE the `except Exception` classify-seam — so the classifier will never see it. The plan does not specify how `UnretryableOutputError`/`StructuredOutputError` (already-typed rebar errors) are handled by `classify_llm_failure`.
findings.location: Exception → resolution_class mapping table, CHANGE_INPUT row; cross-referenced with 'Unwrap/classify' design in 'What' section; and runner.py:351-352 (`except LLMError: raise`)

findings.finding: AC3 requires 'a test enumerates the existing catch/exit-code consumers and asserts their behavior is preserved,' but 'behavior' is not defined — an executor does not know whether to assert (a) only catch-semantics (isinstance check), (b) also that no new attributes on the subclass break existing attribute access, or (c) also that exit-code mappings in CLI consumers are unchanged.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'a test enumerates the existing catch/exit-code consumers and asserts their behavior is preserved'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC3 clause with: 'A test (a) greps `LLMUnavailableError` to enumerate all catch-sites, (b) asserts `isinstance(typed_error, LLMUnavailableError)` for each new subclass, and (c) asserts that any attribute existing consumers read (`message`, `__str__` output, etc.) is present on the subclass and returns a `str`.'

findings.finding: The `retryable` field on `LLMOutcome` is described as 'behaviour-critical' in the mapping table and 'What' section, but no acceptance criterion explicitly verifies that the `retryable` value is correct for each resolution_class. AC1 only checks `resolution_class + diagnostic`; the retryable field is an uncovered deliverable attribute.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 / 'What' section mapping table
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to read: '…maps every RED-suite failure class to the correct `resolution_class`, `retryable`, and `diagnostic`; `retryable` is True iff `resolution_class` in {WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW}.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
